company_profile.name: Ciena Corporation
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Ciena Corporation, a network technology company, provides hardware, software, and services for various network operators in the Americas, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, the Asia Pacific, Japan, and India. It operates through Networking Platforms, Platform Software and Services, Blue Planet Automation Software and Services, and Global Services segments. The Networking Platforms segment consists optical networking, routing, and switching products and services. This segment products include the 6500 Packet-Optical Platform, Waveserver modular interconnect system, the 6500 Reconfigurable Line System, and coherent pluggable transceivers; and the 3000 family of service delivery platforms and the 5000 family of service aggregation, as well as the 8100 Coherent Routing platforms and virtualization software. The Platform Software and Services segment offers navigator network control suite; and software subscription services, consulting, network migration and integration, installation and upgrade support services, and technical support solutions. The Blue Planet Automation Software and Services segment inventory management, orchestration, route optimization and analysis, and unified assurance and analytics software; and sells subscription, installation, support, consulting, and design services related to the Blue Planet automation platform. The Global Services segment provides services for advisory and enablement, implementation, and maintenance, support, and learning activities. Ciena Corporation was incorporated in 1992 and is headquartered in Hanover, Maryland.
company_profile.description_summary:
Ciena provides networking hardware, software, and services to telecommunications operators globally. Its main revenue comes from optical networking, routing, and switching products alongside platform software, automation software, and professional services that help operators manage and optimize their networks.

The company distinguishes itself through comprehensive end-to-end solutions spanning physical infrastructure, software control systems, and automation intelligence, enabling operators to build and manage complex modern networks efficiently.

_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.name: Chimera Investment Corporation
company_profile.type_rationale: Real estate investment trust — P/NAV (P/B) prioritised
company_profile.description: Chimera Investment Corporation operates as a real estate investment trust (REIT) in the United States. It operates in two segments, Investment Portfolio and Residential Origination. The company, through its subsidiaries, invests in, originates, and manages a portfolio of mortgage assets, including residential mortgage loans, non-agency mortgage-backed securities, agency mortgage-backed securities, business purpose loans, investor loans, mortgage servicing rights, and other real estate-related assets. It also invests in investment grade, non-investment grade, and non-rated securities. In addition, the company offers third-party advisory services. The company qualifies as a REIT for federal income tax purposes. As a REIT, it intends to distribute at least 90% of its taxable income as dividends to shareholders. Chimera Investment Corporation was incorporated in 2007 and is headquartered in New York, New York.
company_profile.description_summary: A real estate investment trust that invests in and originates mortgage assets including residential loans, mortgage-backed securities, and servicing rights. It also manages securities across investment grades and provides third-party advisory services. As a REIT, the company distributes at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders as dividends.

_meta.type_rationale: Real estate investment trust — P/NAV (P/B) prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) own properties and are legally required to pay out most of their income as dividends. This means they rarely have much free cash flow left over, so DCF models give misleading results. Instead, we use Price-to-Book (P/B) to estimate the value of the underlying properties, and EV/EBITDA to compare the business to similar property companies.

company_profile.description_summary:

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.description_summary:
# Colgate-Palmolive Company

This company manufactures and sells consumer products globally across two main segments. Oral, Personal and Home Care generates primary revenue through toothpaste, mouthwash, soaps, shampoos, deodorants, and household cleaners sold under brands including Colgate, Palmolive, Tom's of Maine, and Irish Spring. _meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 16.0%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

company_profile.description: Compass Minerals International, Inc. provides essential minerals in the United States, Canada, the United Kingdom, and internationally. The Salt segment produces, markets, and sells sodium chloride and magnesium chloride, including rock salt, mechanically and solar evaporated salt, and brine and flake magnesium chloride products; and purchases and sells potassium chloride and calcium chloride to sell as finished products or to blend with sodium chloride to produce specialty products. This segment provides products for use as a deicer for roadways, consumer, and professional use; as an ingredient in chemical production; for water treatment, human, and animal nutrition; and for various other consumer and industrial uses, as well as records management services. The Plant Nutrition segment produces sulfate of potash specialty fertilizers in various grades that are used in broadcast spreaders, direct application, and liquid fertilizer solutions under the Protassium+ brand name; turf products used by the turf and ornamental markets, as well as for blends used on golf course greens; and organic products. This segment provides its products to distributors and retailers of crop inputs, as well as growers. The company was formerly known as Salt Holdings Corporation and changed its name to Compass Minerals International, Inc. in December 2003. Compass Minerals International, Inc. was founded in 1844 and is headquartered in Overland Park, Kansas.

The company operates across North America and the United Kingdom, serving diverse markets from road maintenance to agriculture and consumer applications.

company_profile.description: The Clorox Company manufactures and markets consumer and professional products worldwide. The company operates through four segments: Health and Wellness, Household, Lifestyle, and International. The Health and Wellness segment offers home care cleaning and disinfecting products, bleach, clog removers, and laundry additives under the Clorox, Clorox2, Pine-Sol, Scentiva, Tilex, Liquid-Plumr, Poett, and Formula 409 brands; professional cleaning and disinfecting products under the CloroxPro and Clorox Healthcare brands; professional food service products under the Hidden Valley brand in the United States. quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Clorox has built a defensible competitive moat through trusted consumer brands, strong pricing power evidenced by its 11.2% profit margin, and consistent cash generation reflected in a 24.92% return on invested capital—well above the 15–20% benchmark for strong businesses—though leverage and flat revenue growth suggest the moat is mature rather than expanding.

metric_assessments.profitability: Clorox's 11.2% profit margin sits above the 8–10% typical for consumer goods companies, showing the business squeezes more profit from each dollar of sales than average peers do. This reflects the pricing power of recognizable brands like Clorox bleach and Hidden Valley, which consumers reach for habitually and don't switch away from lightly. However, margins haven't expanded in recent years, suggesting the company is defending its position rather than growing it—a sign that profitability, while solid, has hit a plateau.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 0.84 and debt-to-EBITDA of 3.52x flag a material weakness: the company has less cash relative to short-term obligations than is comfortable, and its debt load is 3.5 times its annual earnings power, which is elevated for a mature consumer business. While the company generates cash reliably, it lacks the balance-sheet cushion that would allow it to weather a sharp downturn or capitalize on opportunities without stress. This is the core reason the financial-strength score sits at 3 out of 10—the company is solvent and functional but carries more financial risk than ideal.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 0.1% is essentially flat, meaning the business is not expanding and is instead fighting to hold its market share in a slow-moving category. The forward earnings-per-share estimate of –4.6% over five years signals that profit per share is expected to shrink, likely due to inflation pressures and the company's constrained ability to raise prices further without losing volume. That said, a 6 out of 10 score reflects the fact that Clorox is still predictable and cash-generative even in a no-growth environment—the gap to higher scores exists because investors seeking meaningful growth will find little here.
metric_assessments.predictability: Clorox operates in categories where demand is inelastic—people buy bleach and salad dressing regardless of economic cycles—giving the company visibility into future earnings that rivals far-faster-growing tech firms. The 7 out of 10 score acknowledges this stability: a 546% return on equity and 24.92% return on invested capital show the company deploys its capital efficiently to generate consistent returns. The one limiting factor is that predictability without growth means the company is essentially running in place, so while investors can forecast the next year with confidence, they cannot forecast meaningful improvement.
metric_assessments.valuation: An 8 out of 10 valuation score indicates the stock is trading at an attractive price relative to the earnings and returns it generates—likely a reasonable entry point for an income-focused or defensive investor. The market appears to be discounting Clorox's flat growth and elevated leverage, creating a margin of safety for new buyers. The gap to a perfect 10 exists because valuation quality is only as strong as the business's growth trajectory, and at 0.1% revenue growth with negative earnings forecasts ahead, even a cheap price becomes a value trap if the company cannot stabilize its earnings per share.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Clorox is rated Speculative because it combines solid operating quality and profitability with significant financial weakness and negative earnings growth that create uncertainty about future returns. While the company's brands are strong and its cash flows are reliable, the 3.52x debt-to-EBITDA ratio and shrinking per-share earnings mean the business faces real headwinds that could pressure shareholders if conditions worsen. In plain terms: you're buying a good business at a fair price, but the company's heavy debt load and lack of growth mean there's meaningful risk that returns could disappoint.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Clorox is a classic defensive consumer business with trusted household brands, strong pricing power, and a 24.92% return on invested capital that proves it deploys capital efficiently—but those strengths come with real caveats. The company's balance sheet is stretched with debt at 3.52 times earnings, and revenue growth is essentially flat at 0.1% with per-share profits forecast to decline 4.6% over the next five years, meaning the company is not investing in its future but instead managing decline. The main risks are that rising costs will force uncomfortable price hikes that erode volume, or that debt servicing will crowd out reinvestment just as the business needs it most. At current valuations, the stock appears reasonably priced relative to its earnings, which offers some downside protection, but the growth profile is uninspiring and financial stress is a real threat. This is a stock for income-focused investors willing to accept stagnation in exchange for a steady yield, not for those seeking capital appreciation or growth—hold it only if you need defensive exposure and can tolerate the leverage.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: The Clorox Company demonstrates mixed fundamental quality and shows particular strength in profitability, supported by a 24.92% ROIC.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Chipotle's 49% return on equity, 20% return on invested capital, and exceptional predictability signal a durable competitive advantage built on brand loyalty and operational consistency, though constrained unit growth suggests the moat is geographic and market-saturation limited rather than infinitely defensible.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 49.23% return on equity is exceptional—most strong companies post 15–20%—and reflects Chipotle's ability to generate outsized profits from each dollar of shareholder capital, driven by high-margin restaurant operations and disciplined cost control at a 12% net profit margin. The company's pricing power and operational efficiency are clearly working in its favor. However, the growth constraint noted in the data suggests the company is bumping against saturation limits in its core markets, which prevents profitability gains from translating into runaway unit expansion and caps this score just shy of perfect.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 1.23 is typical and healthy—it means Chipotle has $1.23 in liquid assets for every $1 of short-term obligations, well above the minimum 1.0 threshold—but the debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.28 is moderately elevated, indicating the company carries more leverage than a fortress-like balance sheet would carry. The company is financially stable and not at risk, but it has less cushion than a debt-free business would, which is why this score is 'good' rather than 'excellent.' A debt-to-EBITDA below 1.5 would signal exceptional strength; at 2.28, Chipotle is solidly in the middle of the acceptable range.
metric_assessments.growth: Annual revenue growth of 7.4% and projected earnings-per-share growth of 11% over the next five years are both solid but not exceptional—a typical growing company posts 8–12% revenue growth, so Chipotle is on the lower end of that band. The silver lining is that earnings are outpacing revenue, meaning the company is improving its profitability per dollar of sales as it grows, which is a sign of operational leverage. The limiting factor is that growth_constrained is flagged as true, meaning new restaurant openings or market expansion is slowing, so Chipotle is reaching the ceiling of its addressable market in the US and will have to work harder—or expand internationally—to accelerate.
metric_assessments.predictability: A perfect 10/10 score here reflects Chipotle's consistent track record of delivering predictable financial results quarter after quarter, allowing investors and analysts to forecast its earnings with high confidence. The brand is well-established, customer demand is stable, and the operating model is proven and replicable, all of which reduce business surprises. There is no meaningful weakness to cite—this score is as high as it goes because Chipotle has eliminated most sources of unpredictability in its business.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 6/10 rating, Chipotle appears fairly valued rather than cheap or expensive; the stock likely trades at a reasonable multiple relative to its earnings and growth rate, reflecting solid quality without a significant margin of safety or an obvious bargain. The company's strong profitability and predictability justify a premium multiple relative to the broader market, but the slowing growth and moderate leverage mean the stock is not trading at a discount to intrinsic value. For a beginner, this means Chipotle is a 'fair price for fair quality' opportunity, not a screaming buy.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Chipotle earned a 'Safe' rating because it combines strong, stable profitability (8/10) and excellent predictability (10/10)—hallmarks of a low-surprise business—with a solid quality score of 78%, meaning the fundamentals are reliable and the business model is proven. The main constraint is moderate financial strength (6/10) due to elevated leverage and modest growth (7/10) caused by market saturation, so while the business is very safe to own, it won't deliver explosive returns, making it ideal for conservative investors seeking steady, predictable value rather than high-risk upside.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Chipotle is a high-quality restaurant business with exceptional profitability (49% return on equity), rock-solid predictability, and a trusted brand that commands strong pricing power and customer loyalty. The company's main weaknesses are constrained growth—it is hitting saturation in its core US markets—and moderate leverage at 2.28x debt-to-EBITDA, which leaves less financial flexibility than a debt-free competitor would have. Looking ahead, earnings are expected to grow at 11% annually over the next five years, driven more by improving store profitability than by new restaurant openings, which is respectable but not explosive. The stock appears fairly valued at current levels, offering no significant margin of safety but also no red flags, making it a sensible core holding for investors who prioritize reliable earnings and consistency over high growth or deep discounts. This is a 'buy and hold' quality business, not a turnaround or a bargain.

company_profile.description: Claros Mortgage Trust, Inc. operates as a real estate investment trust. company_profile.description_summary:
This real estate investment trust originates senior and subordinate loans on transitional commercial real estate assets across the United States. Its primary revenue comes from originating these mortgage loans on commercial properties undergoing transition or repositioning.

As a REIT, the company avoids federal and state income taxes on distributed earnings, provided it distributes at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders and meets other REIT qualification requirements.

_meta.type_rationale: Real estate investment trust — P/NAV (P/B) prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) own properties and are legally required to pay out most of their income as dividends. This means they rarely have much free cash flow left over, so DCF models give misleading results. Instead, we use Price-to-Book (P/B) to estimate the value of the underlying properties, and EV/EBITDA to compare the business to similar property companies.

company_profile.name: CMS Energy Corporation
company_profile.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
company_profile.description: CMS Energy Corporation operates as an energy company primarily in Michigan. The company operates through three segments: Electric Utility; Gas Utility; and NorthStar Clean Energy. The Electric Utility segment is involved in the generation, purchase, distribution, and sale of electricity. This segment generates electricity through coal, wind, gas, renewable energy, oil, and nuclear sources. Its distribution system comprises 263 miles of high-voltage distribution overhead lines; 4 miles of high-voltage distribution underground lines; 4,619 miles of high-voltage distribution overhead lines; 18 miles of high-voltage distribution underground lines; 82,854 miles of electric distribution overhead lines; 10,027 miles of underground distribution lines; and 1,102 substations. The Gas Utility segment engages in the purchase, transmission, storage, distribution, and sale of natural gas, which includes 2,337 miles of transmission lines; 14 gas storage fields; 28,433 miles of distribution mains; and 8 compressor stations. The NorthStar Clean Energy segment is involved in the independent power production and marketing, including the development and operation of renewable generation. The company serves 1.9 million electric and 1.8 million gas customers, including residential, commercial, and diversified industrial customers. The company was incorporated in 1987 and is headquartered in Jackson, Michigan.
company_profile.description_summary: This energy company operates primarily in Michigan through three segments: Electric Utility, which generates and distributes electricity via coal, wind, gas, nuclear, and renewable sources; Gas Utility, which purchases and distributes natural gas; and NorthStar Clean Energy, which develops renewable power projects. It serves approximately 1.9 million electric and 1.8 million gas customers across residential, commercial, and industrial sectors.

_meta.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
_meta.method_explanation: Utilities like electricity and water companies operate under government regulation, which means their prices and profits are capped but also very stable. They tend to carry a lot of debt because building infrastructure is expensive — but this is normal and expected for the industry. Because earnings are so predictable, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and EV/EBITDA work well. DCF is less useful here because regulated utilities have limited ability to grow beyond what regulators allow.

quality.company_type_note: Banks and REITs carry structural leverage — do not describe financial_strength scores of 7+ as 'moderate concern' for these types.
quality.note: Moat scored by Haiku API — final_score_pct computed out of 50.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: CMS Energy has moderate competitive advantages rooted in regulated utility operations and stable customer bases, but weak returns on invested capital and thin margins limit the strength of its economic moat.

metric_assessments.profitability: At 12.4% net profit margin, CMS is generating a reasonable return on each dollar of revenue—typical for a regulated utility but not exceptional. The company is earning money reliably from its core business, which is what you'd expect from a utility with captive customers and regulated rates. However, the 10.37% return on equity is below the 12-15% threshold that signals genuinely efficient capital deployment, indicating the company isn't generating outsized returns even though it operates in a stable, predictable industry.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: The 0.98 current ratio—meaning current assets barely cover short-term obligations—is concerning and sits below the healthy 1.5+ benchmark for most businesses. More critically, the 6.26x debt-to-EBITDA ratio signals elevated leverage; most utilities operate healthily at 3.5-5.0x, so CMS is carrying substantially more debt relative to the cash it generates. This combination leaves little margin for error if earnings dip or refinancing costs spike, which is a real concern even for a regulated utility.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 11.6% is solid and significantly above the 2-3% typical for mature utilities, suggesting CMS is expanding its customer base or service territory. However, the projected 7.7% earnings per share growth over the next five years trails the revenue growth rate, indicating that the company's bottom-line expansion will be more modest. The data notes growth is constrained, meaning the company likely faces regulatory or operational limits on how much it can expand—a typical ceiling for utilities that can't simply raise prices without approval.
metric_assessments.predictability: Utilities by nature deliver predictable cash flows and earnings because they serve essential services with regulated rates, and CMS's 5/10 score reflects this stability. The company's revenue and profit streams should be quite consistent year to year, which is a major strength for income-focused investors. What's limiting a higher score is that regulated utilities face external risks—rate approvals, regulatory changes, and infrastructure aging—that can create unexpected earnings volatility despite the stable underlying business model.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 5/10 valuation score, CMS appears to be priced at a fair level relative to its fundamentals—neither a bargain nor overpriced. Given the company's modest profitability returns (10.37% ROE), moderate growth outlook (7.7% EPS growth), and elevated debt burden, there's no compelling reason to believe the stock is trading at a discount. An investor paying current prices is essentially getting a stable utility at a reasonable but not attractive price, without the margin of safety that would make this a compelling contrarian opportunity.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: CMS Energy earned a Dangerous rating primarily because of its weak financial strength, specifically a current ratio of 0.98 and a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 6.26x—both well above safe levels even for a utility. While the business itself is stable and regulated (supporting the adequate profitability and predictability scores), the company's debt load relative to cash generation creates meaningful financial stress and reduces flexibility to handle downturns or unexpected costs. quality_narrative.investment_narrative: CMS Energy is a regulated utility that generates steady, predictable profits from essential services—the business does what you'd expect, with reasonable margins and revenue growth. _meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 15.0%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Core Natural Resources, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, produces, sells, and exports metallurgical and thermal coals in the United States and internationally. It operates through the High CV Thermal; Metallurgical; Powder River Basin (PRB); and Core Marine Terminal segments. The High CV Thermal segment consists of Pennsylvania Mining Complex and the West Elk mine located in Colorado. The Metallurgical segment consists of Leer, Leer South, Beckley, Mountain Laurel, and Itmann coal mines in West Virginia. The PRB segment consists of Black Thunder and Coal Creek surface mining complexes located in Wyoming. The Core Marine Terminal segment consists of coal export terminal operations in the Port of Baltimore. The company was formerly known as CONSOL Energy Inc. and changed its name to Core Natural Resources, Inc. in January 2025. Core Natural Resources, Inc. was founded in 1864 and is headquartered in Canonsburg, Pennsylvania.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company produces and sells metallurgical and thermal coals across the United States and internationally, operating multiple underground and surface mines in Pennsylvania, Colorado, West Virginia, and Wyoming. It also runs a coal export terminal in Baltimore, generating revenue from coal extraction and export operations.

The company distinguishes itself through its diversified portfolio spanning high-CV thermal coal, metallurgical coal, and Powder River Basin operations, alongside integrated export terminal infrastructure that enables direct market access.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: CNX Resources Corporation, an independent natural gas and midstream company, engages in the acquisition, exploration, development, and production of natural gas properties in the Appalachian Basin. The company operates in two segments, Shale and Coalbed Methane (CBM). It produces and sells pipeline quality natural gas primarily for gas wholesalers. The company owns rights to extract natural gas from shale formations in Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Ohio, as well as rights to extract natural gas from other Shale and shallow oil and gas formations primarily in Illinois, Indiana, New York, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Virginia, and West Virginia. In addition, the company designs, builds, and operates natural gas gathering systems to move natural gas from the wellhead to interstate pipelines or other local sales points; owns or operates approximately 2,600 miles of natural gas gathering pipelines as well as various natural gas processing facilities. Further, it offers turn-key solutions for water sourcing, delivery and disposal for its natural gas operations and supplies solutions for water sourcing as well as delivery and disposal for third parties. The company was formerly known as CONSOL Energy Inc. and changed its name to CNX Resources Corporation in November 2017. CNX Resources Corporation was founded in 1860 and is based in Canonsburg, Pennsylvania.
company_profile.description_summary:
This independent natural gas and midstream company acquires, explores, develops, and produces natural gas from properties across the Appalachian Basin and beyond. It generates revenue primarily by extracting and selling pipeline-quality natural gas, while also operating gathering systems and processing facilities that move gas from wellheads to interstate pipelines.

The company distinguishes itself through integrated operations spanning shale and coalbed methane production, midstream infrastructure ownership, and water management solutions for both its operations and third-party clients.

bull_points: Q4 2025 EPS beat estimates by 70%, posting $0.68/share vs $0.40 consensus, with full-year 2025 revenues up 18.1% YoY to $1.73B, signaling strong near-term operational momentum that could drive positive estimate revisions heading into Q1 2026 earnings.

Expanded $4.45B share repurchase authorization with no expiration provides a direct per-share value creation lever in the near term, especially if CNX deploys capital aggressively while natural gas prices remain supported by the current hedged production strategy.

2026 production guidance of 605-620 Bcfe anchored by extensive hedging positions management to deliver predictable cash flows and minimize downside from commodity volatility through the next 3-6 months, supporting margin defense in an uncertain macro environment.
bear_points: Long-term debt jumped 20% YoY to $2.21B as of Dec 31, 2025, while cash collapsed to $0.8M from $17.2M, creating a weakened balance sheet that limits financial flexibility and raises refinancing risk if capital markets tighten in the near term.

Zacks Rank #4 (Sell) rating reflects analyst skepticism despite the earnings beat, with multiple sources noting limited upside at current prices and heightened leverage risk that could pressure the stock if natural gas prices decline over the next 2-3 quarters.

Cash from operations of $1.03B in 2025 disappointed relative to the $646M free cash flow generated, with 2026 FCF guidance only $550M—down from $646M—signaling slowing cash generation momentum as production remains flat while capital intensity persists.

bull_points: Exceptional profitability with 52.7% net profit margin and 28.08% ROE demonstrates CNX's ability to convert commodity revenues into shareholder returns at scale; the intrinsic value of $78.91 vs current price of $33.25 reflects a 57.9% margin of safety, indicating substantial long-term upside if operational leverage materializes.

Disciplined capital allocation prioritizing balance sheet strength and shareholder returns via the expanded buyback program, combined with Deep Utica program execution and production guidance holding flat, positions CNX to grow EPS 16.5% annually through 2031 despite modest volume growth.

Strong hedging portfolio and flat production guidance (605-620 Bcfe) provide cash flow visibility and resilience across commodity price cycles, allowing CNX to fund the $550M+ annual FCF guidance sustainably while simultaneously returning capital—a rare combination among energy producers.
bear_points: Financial strength score of just 4/10 with current ratio of 0.44 and debt-to-EBITDA of 1.16 reveals structural balance sheet weakness that constrains long-term growth; the 16.5% EPS growth rate was explicitly moderated downward from 22% potential due to this financial stress, limiting the company's ability to invest in transformational growth.

Flat production guidance through 2026 and management's explicit emphasis on stability over volume growth suggest CNX faces structural headwinds in a low-growth energy market; with only 6/10 growth quality score, the company lacks the operational leverage to justify the 57.9% margin of safety beyond a cyclical rebound scenario.

New management team with Alan Shepard transitioning to CEO and Everett Good taking CFO role presents execution risk, as Simply Wall St notes regulatory uncertainty around tax credits could materially impact free cash flow predictability—the core thesis behind capital returns—over a 5-year horizon.
